title: Price discrimination
description: Practice what you have learned about price discrimination, including first degree (a.k.a, perfect price discrimination) in this exercise

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best defines perfect price discrimination?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A firm charges all buyers their entire willingness to pay.
A firm charges all buyers different prices based on varying costs of production.
A firm charges some buyers one price and other buyers another price, but some consumer surplus remains.
A firm charges some buyers one price and other buyers another price, but there is deadweight loss.
A firm charges a single price which is greater than the marginal cost of production.

assessment_items.question_content:
Blammo Industries is a profit maximizer that sells custom made hats and is the only producer in a market. It charges some customers more than other customers because the hats bought by some buyers cost Blammo more to produce than the hats purchased by other buyers.

**Based on this information, each statement below is true EXCEPT**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Blammo Industries is a price discriminating monopolist.
Blammo industries does not have a constant marginal cost curve
Blammo Industries has a downward sloping demand curve.
Blammo industries will produce the quantity where marginal cost equals marginal revenue
Blammo Industries operates in an industry with barriers to entry.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes perfect price discrimination?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The ability to charge different buyers their entire willingness to pay for goods with identical production costs
The ability to charge one group of buyers a different price than another group of buyers, even though the purchased goods have identical production costs
The ability to set a single price for a good based on buyer characteristics.
The illegal practice of charging some groups of buyers one price and other groups of buyers a different price
The ability to charge each customer the marginal cost of production.

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph shown here illustrates the demand curve, marginal revenue curve, and the cost curves for a profit-maximizing monopolist with constant costs.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/54493f7cd00925319c011b5d565c755d1ea1c563)

**If this monopolist can price discriminate, what is the value of its profit?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The area that represents profit for a perfect price discriminating monopolist is the area under the demand curve and above average total cost up to the quantity produced.

A perfect price discriminating monopolist’s demand curve is its marginal revenue curve because it can charge each buyer their entire willingness to pay.  
A perfect price discriminating monopolist chooses a quantity of $6$ when the marginal cost is $\$8$ because $D=MR=MC$ when quantity equals $6$.
Profit is the area under the demand curve up to a quantity of $6$. The area for a triangle is $\dfrac{1}{2}\times \text{base}\times\text{height}$.
Profit equals $\$36$, as illustrated in the shaded area in the graph below:

![A graph illustrates cost, price, and quantity relationships in a market structure, with cost in dollars on the vertical axis (ranging from 0 to 20) and Quantity on the horizontal axis (ranging from 0 to Q 4). The graph includes three curves: Demand (D), Marginal Revenue (M R), and Average Total Cost equals Marginal Cost (A T C equals M C), which is a horizontal line at 8. The downward-sloping Demand curve starts at a cost of 20 and intersects the Quantity axis at Q 4. The M R curve is also downward-sloping, starting below the Demand curve at 20 and intersecting the Quantity axis before Q4. The A T C equals M C curve is a horizontal line at 8, intersecting the Demand curve at 6 units of quantity. Key intersections are marked. The Demand curve intersects the vertical dashed line at 3 units and a price of 14. At 5 units of quantity, the Demand curve is at a price of 10, and the M R curve intersects at this point. At 6 units of quantity, the Demand curve intersects the A T C equals M C curve, establishing equilibrium at 8. Dashed vertical and horizontal lines connect these points to the axes for clarity, indicating quantities (3, 5, and 6) and corresponding prices (14, 10, and 8).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8e0bbef05ffbd4bd1136b14e0a6aa066b7fe5b1b)

$\begin{align} \text{Profit} &=\dfrac{1}{2}\times  \text{base} \times \text{height}\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 6 \times (\$20-\$8)\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 6 \times \$12\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2}\times \$72\\\\
&=\$36\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph shown here illustrates the demand curve, marginal revenue curve, and the cost curves for a profit-maximizing monopolist with constant costs.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6f00dceede16d5100ea19138b6ab15ae0a14846d)

**If this monopolist can price discriminate, what is the value of its total revenue?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The area that represents total revenue for a perfect price discriminating monopolist is the area under the demand curve and up to the quantity produced.

The shape that represents the area of total revenue is a quadrilateral that looks like this:

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c13f03b53b11a2055024bcaf5eada4462ce0e635)

Note that this kind of quadrilateral is a triangle plus a rectangle:

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c52f1d1db2a033d20747ed8ca8ceb25bb45179ee)

A perfect price discriminating monopolist’s demand curve is its marginal revenue curve because it can charge each buyer their entire willingness to pay. A perfect price discriminating monopolist chooses a quantity of $4$ when the marginal cost is $\$10$ because $D=MR=MC$ when quantity equals $4$.

The area that represents profit is the shaded area shown in this graph:

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/81d14200bdf82ec01d366e2be347b09f5a42136a)

To find the area of this shape, calculate the area of the triangle shown in this graph and add it to the area of the rectangle shown in this graph. Note that the triangle represents profit and the rectangle represents total costs.

![A line graph illustrates the relationship between cost, price, and quantity for a firm. The graph has a vertical axis labeled Cost, in dollars, and a horizontal axis labeled Quantity. Three lines and one shaded area are displayed. The horizontal line labeled A T C equals M C is drawn at a cost of 10 dollars. The downward-sloping line labeled D represents demand, and the downward-sloping line labeled M R represents marginal revenue. Key points include: The A T C equals M C line intersects the D line at a quantity of 4. The D line intersects the Y-axis at a price of 14 dollars. The M R line intersects the X-axis at a quantity of 7. Dashed vertical lines extend from quantities 2, 4, and 7 to the X-axis, and dashed horizontal lines extend from costs 7 and 10 to the Y-axis. A shaded area below the price of 10 dollars and above the M R line, from quantity 2 to 4, represents total revenue or profit-related regions.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6126541f1b0d81fa68af9b80dbcb56d1df8c38a7)

The area of the triangle is $\$8$:

$\begin{align} \text{area of profit triangle} &=\dfrac{1}{2} \times\text{base} \times \text{height}\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times (\$14-\$10)\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times \$4\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \$16\\\\
&=\$8 \end{align}$


The area of the rectangle is $\$40$:

$\begin{align} \text{area of total cost} &= \text{base}\times \text{height}\\\\
&=4\times\$10\\\\
&=\$40
\end{align}$


Total revenue is $\$48$:

$\$8 + \$40= \$48$

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph shown here illustrates the demand curve, marginal revenue curve, and the cost curves for a profit-maximizing monopolist with constant costs.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6f00dceede16d5100ea19138b6ab15ae0a14846d)

**If this monopolist can price discriminate, what is the value of its producer surplus?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The area that represents producer surplus for a perfect price discriminating monopolist is the area under the demand curve and up to the quantity produced and above the marginal cost curve.

The light shaded area in this graph represents producer surplus.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6126541f1b0d81fa68af9b80dbcb56d1df8c38a7)

The area of the triangle is $\$8$:

$\begin{align} \text{area of producer surplus} &=\dfrac{1}{2} \times\text{base} \times \text{height}\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times (\$14-\$10)\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times \$4\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \$16\\\\
&=\$8 \end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph shown here illustrates the demand curve, marginal revenue curve, and the cost curves for a profit-maximizing monopolist with constant costs and no fixed costs that cannot currently price discriminate.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6126541f1b0d81fa68af9b80dbcb56d1df8c38a7)

**If conditions change and this firm can perfectly price discriminate, what is the value of deadweight loss if this firm can perfectly price discriminate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

The graph shown here illustrates the demand curve, marginal revenue curve, and the cost curves for a profit-maximizing monopolist with constant costs.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/839f4797cb51b7c490af292e24742862cc12c675)

**What area represents the firm's producer surplus?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A perfect price discriminating monopolist will charge each buyer their entire willingness to pay. Therefore, unlike for a single price monopolist, the entire area under the demand curve up to the quantity produced and above average total cost represents profit.

The marginal revenue curve for a price discriminating firm is different than the marginal revenue curve for a single-price monopolist. The marginal revenue curve for a perfect price discriminating monopolist is its demand curve because it doesn’t have to lower its price to all buyers in order to sell more, it can charge each buyer their willingness to pay.

The shape of the area representing profit is a rectangle for a single price monopolist, but the shape of the area representing profit is a triangle for a perfect price discriminating firm with constant costs.
The perfect price discriminating firm produces $Q_3$ because that is the quantity where demand equals marginal cost.

The area that represents profit for a perfect price discriminating firm is  $P_wfP_z$:

![A line graph depicts the relationship between cost, price, and quantity for a firm. The graph has a vertical axis labeled Cost, in dollars, and a horizontal axis labeled Quantity. Three lines and a shaded area are displayed. The horizontal line labeled A T C equals M C is drawn at price P z. The downward-sloping line labeled D equals M R represents demand and marginal revenue. Key points and intersections include: The D equals M R line intersects the Y-axis at price P w and the X-axis at quantity Q 4. The A T C equals M C line intersects the D equals M R line at point f at quantity Q 3. Dashed vertical lines extend from quantities Q 1, Q 2, and Q 3 to the X-axis and connect to the D equals M R and A T C equals M C lines at points a, b, c, d, e, and f. Dashed horizontal lines extend from price levels P x, P y, and P z to the Y-axis. A green area is enclosed between the D equals M R line, the A T C equals M C line, and the vertical lines extending from quantities Q 1 and Q 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c6589209975513d83792ac8f860e4af539175e36)

choices.content: $P_wfP_z$
$P_xadP_z$
$P_wfQ_30$
$P_waP_x$

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph shown here illustrates the demand curve, marginal revenue curve, and the cost curves for a profit-maximizing monopolist with constant costs.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6168e25623c7980b74ce3b4c02d8b0aa05390348)

**If this monopolist can price discriminate, what is the value of its total revenue?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A perfectly price discriminating monopolist chooses the quantity where marginal cost equals demand because the perfect price discriminating monopolist’s marginal revenue curve is its demand curve. 
A perfect price discriminating monopolist will charge each buyer their entire willingness to pay. Therefore, unlike for a single price monopolist, the entire area under the demand curve up to the quantity produced represents total revenue.

The shape of the area representing profit is a rectangle for a single price monopolist, but the shape of the area representing total revenue is a quadrilateral like the one shown here:

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c13f03b53b11a2055024bcaf5eada4462ce0e635)
The perfect price discriminating firm produces $Q_3$ because that is the quantity where demand equals marginal cost.
The area that represents total revenue for a perfect price discriminating firm is  $P_akQ_30$:

![A line graph illustrates the relationship between cost, price, and quantity for a firm. The graph has a vertical axis labeled Cost, in dollars, and a horizontal axis labeled Quantity. Three lines and a shaded area are displayed. The horizontal line labeled A T C equals M C is drawn at price P c. The downward-sloping line labeled D represents demand, and the downward-sloping line labeled M R represents marginal revenue. Key intersections and labels include: The D line intersects the Y-axis at price P a and the X-axis at quantity Q 4. The A T C equals M C line intersects the D line at point i at quantity Q 2. The M R line intersects the X-axis at quantity Q 4. Points f, j, k, i, h, g, l, and m mark intersections between the lines and the dashed horizontal and vertical lines. Dashed horizontal lines extend from price levels P a, P b, P c, P d, and P e to the Y-axis. Dashed vertical lines extend from quantities Q 1, Q 2, Q 3, and Q 4 to the X-axis. An area is enclosed between the D line, the A T C equals M C line, and the vertical lines extending from Q 1 and Q 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/636a284a7c387144cb64a447e46dcb487dfdcf89)

choices.content: $P_akQ_30$
$P_bfQ_10$
$PakPc$
$P_amQ_40$
$P_bjfP_C$

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph shown here illustrates the demand curve, marginal revenue curve, and the cost curves for a profit-maximizing monopolist with constant costs.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6168e25623c7980b74ce3b4c02d8b0aa05390348)

**What area represents deadweight loss (“DWL”) if this firm chooses a single price and what is the deadweight loss if this firm can perfectly price discriminate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: $DWL=fkj$ for a single price monopolist; $DWL=0$ for perfect price discriminating monopolist.
$DWL=P_afP_b$ for a single price monopolist; $DWL=klm$ for a perfect price discriminating monopolist
$DWL= 0$ for a single price monopolist; $DWL=0$ for a perfect price discriminating monopolist
$DWL=kih$ for a single price monopolist; $DWL=fjk$ for a perfect price discriminating monopolist.
$DWL=klm$ for a single price monopolist; $DWL=fjk$ for a perfect price discriminating monopolist.

assessment_items.question_content:
**All of the following are allocatively efficient EXCEPT which option?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A single-price monopolist
A perfectly price discriminating monopolist
A perfectly competitive firm earning positive economic profit in the short run
A perfectly competitive firm earning normal economic profit in the long run
A perfectly competitive firm earning negative economic profit in the short run.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is true about a perfect price discriminating monopolist?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It produces the same quantity as a perfectly competitive firm with the same costs.
It creates deadweight loss by producing a quantity where price is greater than marginal cost.
It produces a quantity that maximizes consumer surplus.
It minimizes average total cost
It charges the same price to all buyers

assessment_items.question_content:
**All of the following are necessary for a firm to price discriminate except**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a monopoly
the ability to identify a buyer’s willingness to pay
the ability to prevent resale
the ability to control price
differences in the price elasticities of different consumers
